title: 50,000 AI-music tracks are now uploaded to Deezer every day - Music Ally
text:
In January this year, streaming service Deezer said that
around 10,000 fully AI-generated tracks were being uploaded to its service every day
– 10% of all music uploads.
By April
this figure had grown to more than 20,000 AI tracks a day
, 18% of all uploads. By September
the number had swelled to over 30,000 tracks a day, representing 28% of all uploads
.
This morning, Deezer has provided its latest update. It says that roughly 50,000 fully AI-generated tracks are now uploaded every day, accounting for 34% of all music delivered to the service. More than a third.
The new figures were published alongside the findings from a survey Deezer commissioned from Ipsos, covering 9,000 people in eight countries: the US, Japan, UK, Germany, France, Brazil, the Netherlands and Canada.
The most eye-catching finding is that the survey involved respondents listening to two fully AI-generated tracks and one human song, then guessing which ones were AI.
97% failed the test. The results varied by country a bit, with 5% of Brits successfully identifying the AI tracks, but only 1% of Japanese respondents. But the overall results are a striking reflection of the current state of GenAI music.
Why does this matter? Deezer’s survey sheds more light on that too. While 66% of music-streaming users say they’d listen to fully AI-generated music at least once out of curiosity, 45% said they’d like it filtered out of their streaming service, and 40% said they would skip it without listening “if they came across it”.
The key thing there, of course, is that they’d do this only if they knew it was full-AI. 80% said they think it should be clearly labelled as such for listeners, which support’s Deezer’s policy of doing exactly that – using its in-house AI-detection tool to provide said labels.
“The survey results clearly show that people care about music and want to know if they’re listening to AI or human made tracks or not,” said Deezer CEO Alexis Lanternier.
“There’s also no doubt that there are concerns about how AI-generated music will affect the livelihood of artists, music creation and that AI companies shouldn’t be allowed to train their models on copyrighted material.”
That refers to two more findings from the survey: that 70% of respondents agreed that fully AI-generated music “threatens the livelihood of current and future artists / musicians / composers” and that 65% agreed that AI-music firms shouldn’t be allowed to train their models on copyrighted music.
The wording of this question differs from the prevailing music-industry view, which is that they should be allowed to train on copyrighted music – but only with proper licensing deals to do so.
One more finding from the survey: 69% of people agreed that “payouts for 100% AI-generated music should be lower than for human-made music”. That’s not a measure that Deezer or any of its rivals have taken… yet.
Deezer does exclude GenAI music from its royalty payouts if it detects streaming fraud – for example bots being used to inflate plays of those tracks. It also keeps GenAI music out of its algorithmic recommendations and editorial playlists.
Will it go further? “Potential future actions, including updating our supplier policy and removing / demonetising content need to be based on careful consideration,” said Deezer in its announcement of the findings. A strong hint that we should watch this space.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
